6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For about a decade, TypeScript vs JavaScript was a taste debate. Like tabs vs spaces, or vim vs everything else. You picked a side, you argued about it in code reviews, and deep down everyone knew both camps shipped perfectly fine software.
That debate is over. Not because one side won the argument, but because the argument itself became irrelevant. The question is no longer “do you like types?” The question is “who is writing your code?”
And increasingly, the answer is: not entirely you.
The stat that ended the discussion
In August 2025, something happened that hadn’t happened in over a decade of GitHub’s language rankings. TypeScript overtook both Python and JavaScript to become the most used language on GitHub by monthly contributors.
Not “fastest growing.” Not “most loved in a survey.” Most used. GitHub’s Octoverse 2025 report counted 2,636,006 monthly TypeScript contributors, up more than a million in a single year. That’s 66% year over year growth for a language that’s been around since 2012. Mature languages do not grow 66% in a year. Something external pushed it.
GitHub was pretty direct about what that something was. The same report notes that 80% of new developers on…
